topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly thrown stone. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously researched piece on urban planning, the one I’d poured months into, was attributed to 'Elias Vance.' My stomach tightened immediately. I clicked the link to the published article. The prose was familiar, the arguments identical, yet the framing felt subtly shifted, a different emphasis that didn't align with my own voice. I reread the introduction, my jaw clenching. Who was Elias Vance? I started tracing the publication history, pulling up past interviews and public appearances. Nothing. It was as if this entire existence of Vance was a carefully constructed facade designed to mask the true author. A cold knot formed in my chest, and I began rapidly cross-referencing every detail, searching for a thread that would unravel this deception.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glow reflecting in my wide eyes. The notification popped up: 'Your submission, 'The Future City,' has been published under the name Julian Reed.' My breath hitched. I physically leaned back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. I didn't move for a full minute, just letting the sheer absurdity of the situation wash over me. I pulled up the original manuscript file, comparing the language, the specific anecdotes I’d used. Every sentence matched, yet the ownership was entirely foreign. A slow, deliberate shake ran through my hands. I picked up my phone, my fingers hovering over the contact information for the editor, my voice sounding unnervingly steady as I prepared to ask the first, carefully worded question.

[story 3]

The moment I saw the attribution, a sudden, sharp chill ran down my spine, starting at the base of my neck and spreading outward. I reread the published piece three times, my gaze darting back and forth between the text and the name printed beneath it. It felt like a deliberate misdirection, a carefully placed decoy. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small office, my movements becoming jerky and restless. My palms grew damp. I walked over to the window, looking out at the bustling street below, feeling an intense pressure building behind my eyes. This wasn't an oversight; this felt calculated. I needed proof, tangible evidence to settle the unsettling feeling that something fundamental about my work had been stolen.
